papers.title: Efficient Parallelization of Message Passing Neural Network Potentials for Large-scale Molecular Dynamics
papers.abstract: Machine learning potentials have achieved great success in accelerating atomistic simulations. Many of them relying on atom-centered local descriptors are natural for parallelization. More recent message passing neural network (MPNN) models have demonstrated their superior accuracy and become increasingly popular. However, efficiently parallelizing MPNN models across multiple nodes remains challenging, limiting their practical applications in large-scale simulations. Here, we propose an efficient parallel algorithm for MPNN models, in which additional data communication is minimized among local atoms only in each MP layer without redundant computation, thus scaling linearly with the layer number. Integrated with our recursively embedded atom neural network model, this algorithm demonstrates excellent strong scaling and weak scaling behaviors in several benchmark systems. This approach enables massive molecular dynamics simulations on MPNN models as fast as on strictly local models for over 100 million atoms, vastly extending the applicability of the MPNN potential to an unprecedented scale. This general parallelization framework can empower various MPNN models to efficiently simulate very large and complex systems.

papers.title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
papers.abstract: The core of molecular dynamics simulation fundamentally lies in the interatomic potential. Traditional empirical potentials lack accuracy, while first-principles methods are computationally prohibitive. Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) promise near-quantum accuracy at linear cost, but existing models still face challenges in efficiency and stability. We presents Machine Learning Advances Neural Network (MLANet), an efficient and robust graph neural network framework. MLANet introduces a dual-path dynamic attention mechanism for geometry-aware message passing and a multi-perspective pooling strategy to construct comprehensive system representations. This design enables highly accurate modeling of atomic environments while achieving exceptional computational efficiency, making high-fidelity simulations more accessible. Tested across a wide range of datasets spanning diverse systems, including organic molecules (e.g., QM7, MD17), periodic inorganic materials (e.g., Li-containing crystals), two-dimensional materials (e.g., bilayer graphene, black phosphorus), surface catalytic reactions (e.g., formate decomposition), and charged systems, MLANet maintains competitive prediction accuracy while its computational cost is markedly lower than mainstream equivariant models, and it enables stable long-time molecular dynamics simulations. MLANet provides an efficient and practical tool for large-scale, high-accuracy atomic simulations.
papers.reason: Problem: The paper addresses the problem of developing machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) to enable molecular dynamics simulations with near-quantum accuracy at linear computational cost, explicitly stating that 'first-principles methods are computationally prohibitive' and MLIPs 'promise near-quantum accuracy at linear cost'. This directly aligns with the user's idea of 'simulat[ing] molecular dynamics for lithium-ion battery cathode materials without full DFT at each timestep' by training a neural network potential. | Method: Both the user's idea and the paper propose training a neural network potential using a message-passing architecture on DFT-labeled atomic configurations to simulate molecular dynamics. The paper explicitly mentions using a 'graph neural network framework' with 'geometry-aware message passing' and training on 'ab initio datasets' (including a 'Li-containing subset of the Mptrj dataset') to enable 'stable long-time molecular dynamics simulations', which aligns very closely with the user's core methodology. | Domain: The user's idea explicitly mentions 'lithium-ion battery cathode materials' and the paper evaluates MLANet on 'Li-containing crystals' from the Mptrj dataset, which is a direct overlap in the specific application domain. Both focus on simulating molecular dynamics for materials relevant to lithium-ion batteries using machine learning interatomic potentials. | Contribution: The paper's core contribution, MLANet, is a graph neural network framework for machine learning interatomic potentials that uses a message-passing architecture and is trained on DFT-labeled atomic configurations to simulate molecular dynamics. This directly matches the user's research idea, including the specific application to 'Li-containing crystals' (lithium-ion battery cathode materials) and the goal of simulating molecular dynamics without full DFT at each timestep.

papers.title: Towards Universal Neural Network Potential for Material Discovery Applicable to Arbitrary Combination of 45 Elements
papers.abstract: Computational material discovery is under intense study owing to its ability to explore the vast space of chemical systems. Neural network potentials (NNPs) have been shown to be particularly effective in conducting atomistic simulations for such purposes. However, existing NNPs are generally designed for narrow target materials, making them unsuitable for broader applications in material discovery. To overcome this issue, we have developed a universal NNP called PreFerred Potential (PFP), which is able to handle any combination of 45 elements. Particular emphasis is placed on the datasets, which include a diverse set of virtual structures used to attain the universality. We demonstrated the applicability of PFP in selected domains: lithium diffusion in LiFeSO${}_4$F, molecular adsorption in metal-organic frameworks, an order-disorder transition of Cu-Au alloys, and material discovery for a Fischer-Tropsch catalyst. They showcase the power of PFP, and this technology provides a highly useful tool for material discovery.

The paper explicitly states it developed a 'universal NNP called PreFerred Potential (PFP), which is able to handle any combination of 45 elements' and demonstrated its applicability to 'lithium diffusion in LiFeSO4F' using MD simulations. The core methodology of using a message-passing architecture (TeaNet, a GNN) for the NNP and training on DFT data for MD simulations is highly similar, with the paper providing a specific implementation and application to lithium-ion battery materials. | Domain: The user's research idea explicitly mentions 'lithium-ion battery cathode materials' and the paper dedicates a section to 'Lithium diffusion in LiFeSO4F', which is identified as a 'cathode material for lithium-ion batteries'. This directly aligns with the user's idea, and the paper explicitly demonstrates its application to 'lithium diffusion in LiFeSO4F' (a lithium-ion battery cathode material) without full DFT at each timestep, as detailed in the 'Lithium diffusion' and 'Molecular dynamics simulation of lithium diffusion' sections.

papers.title: EDIS: A Simulation Software for Dynamic Ion Intercalation/Deintercalation Processes in Electrode Materials
papers.abstract: As the core determinant of lithium-ion battery performance, electrode materials play a crucial role in defining the battery's capacity, cycling stability, and durability. During charging and discharging, electrode materials undergo complex ion intercalation and deintercalation processes, accompanied by defect formation and structural evolution. However, the microscopic mechanisms underlying processes such as cation disordering, lattice oxygen loss, and stage structure formation phenomena are still not fully understood. To address these challenges, we have developed the Electrode Dynamic Ion Intercalation/Deintercalation Simulator (EDIS), a software platform designed to simulate the dynamic processes of ion intercalation and deintercalation in electrode materials. Leveraging high-precision machine learning potentials, EDIS can efficiently model structural evolution and lithium-ion diffusion behavior under various states of charge and discharge, achieving accuracy approaching that of quantum mechanical methods in relevant chemical spaces. The software supports quantitative analysis of how variations in lithium-ion concentration and distribution affect lithium-ion transport properties, enables evaluation of the impact of structural defects, and allows for tracking of both structural evolution and transport characteristics during continuous cycling. EDIS is versatile and can be extended to sodium-ion batteries and related systems. By enabling in-depth analysis of these microscopic processes, EDIS provides a robust theoretical tool for mechanistic studies and the rational design of high-performance electrode materials for next-generation lithium-ion batteries.

papers.title: AI-Driven Accelerated Discovery of Intercalation-type Cathode Materials for Magnesium Batteries
papers.abstract: Magnesium-ion batteries hold promise as future energy storage solution, yet current Mg cathodes are challenged by low voltage and specific capacity. Herein, we present an AI-driven workflow for discovering high-performance Mg cathode materials. Utilizing the common characteristics of various ionic intercalation-type electrodes, we design and train a Crystal Graph Convolutional Neural Network model that can accurately predicts electrode voltages for various ions with mean absolute errors (MAE) between 0.25 and 0.33 V. By deploying the trained model to stable Mg compounds from Materials Project and GNoME AI dataset, we identify 160 high voltage structures out of 15,308 candidates with voltages above 3.0 V and volumetric capacity over 800 Ah/L. We further train a precise NequIP model to facilitate accurate and rapid simulations of Mg ionic conductivity. From the 160 high voltage structures, the machine learning molecular dynamics simulations have selected 23 cathode materials with both high energy density and high ionic conductivity. This AI-driven workflow dramatically boosts the efficiency and precision of material discovery for multivalent ion batteries, paving the way for advanced Mg battery development.

The core problem of using ML potentials derived from DFT to accelerate MD simulations for battery materials is highly similar, differing mainly in the specific ion (lithium vs. magnesium) and the primary property being screened (general MD vs. ionic conductivity). | Method: The paper's methodology is very similar to the user's idea. existing_examples.name: Machine Learning Potentials (MLPs) in Materials Science
existing_examples.description: The general field of using machine learning, particularly neural networks, to learn interatomic potentials from ab initio data (like DFT) to enable large-scale and long-time molecular dynamics simulations. This is a very active and well-established research area.

reality_check.assessment: The idea describes a well-established and highly active research area within computational materials science and machine learning. Training neural network potentials, particularly those with message-passing architectures (graph neural networks), on DFT-labeled atomic configurations to accelerate molecular dynamics simulations is a core methodology in modern materials research. This approach is widely used for various materials, including those relevant to lithium-ion batteries, to overcome the computational cost of direct DFT-MD. Several prominent codes and frameworks already implement this exact methodology.

matched_sections.paper_title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: The core of molecular dynamics simulation fundamentally lies in the interatomic potential. Traditional empirical potentials lack accuracy, while first-principles methods are computationally prohibitive. Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) promise near-quantum accuracy at linear cost, but existing models still face challenges in efficiency and stability.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper address the problem of simulating molecular dynamics with high accuracy and efficiency, specifically by overcoming the computational limitations of first-principles methods like DFT.

matched_sections.paper_title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Although DFT provides rigorous first-principles precision, its prohibitive O(N ³ ) computational scaling restricts simulations to systems below 1,000 atoms and picosecond timescales, making large-scale phenomena like defect dynamics or catalytic reactions computationally intractable [15, 16]. The advent of machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) has revolutionized this landscape, offering near-DFT accuracy with linear computational scaling.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states the problem of DFT's computational cost for MD simulations and positions MLIPs as a solution, which directly aligns with the user's goal of simulating MD 'without full DFT at each timestep'.

matched_sections.paper_title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: We presents Machine Learning Advances Neural Network (MLANet), an efficient and robust graph neural network framework. MLANet introduces a dual-path dynamic attention mechanism for geometry-aware message passing and a multi-perspective pooling strategy to construct comprehensive system representations.

matched_sections.paper_title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: The advent of machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) has revolutionized this landscape, offering near-DFT accuracy with linear computational scaling. By training on ab initio datasets, MLIPs infer energies and forces through data-driven representations, bypassing explicit electronic structure calculations[17, 18]. Early invariant MLIPs like CGCNN[19], SchNet[20] and DimeNet[21] relied on handcrafted geometric features (e.g., interatomic distances, angles), but their fixed descriptors struggled to distinguish configurations with identical local geometries[22, 23].
matched_sections.reason: The user's idea of 'Training a neural network potential... on DFT-labeled atomic configurations' directly corresponds to the paper's description of MLIPs being 'trained on ab initio datasets' (DFT being a first-principles ab initio method) to infer energies and forces.

matched_sections.paper_title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
matched_sections.heading: Datasets
matched_sections.text_snippet: Mptrj[43]:The MPtrj dataset comprises 1,580,395 structures, 1,580,395 energy values, 7,944,833 magnetic moments, 49,295,660 force entries, and 14,223,555 stress components. The structures and corresponding labels were parsed from all GGA/GGA+U static/relaxation trajectories in the Materials Project database (version 2022.9), employing a selection methodology designed to exclude incompatible calculations and duplicate structures. For model testing, we extracted all structures containing lithium (Li). The resulting training set consists of 219,033 structures, while the test set contains 1,449 structures.

matched_sections.paper_title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) promise near-quantum accuracy at linear cost, but existing models still face challenges in efficiency and stability. We presents Machine Learning Advances Neural Network (MLANet), an efficient and robust graph neural network framework. MLANet introduces a dual-path dynamic attention mechanism for geometry-aware message passing and a multi-perspective pooling strategy to construct comprehensive system representations. This design enables highly accurate modeling of atomic environments while achieving exceptional computational efficiency, making high-fidelity simulations more accessible.

matched_sections.paper_title: Towards Universal Neural Network Potential for Material Discovery Applicable to Arbitrary Combination of 45 Elements
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Neural network potentials (NNPs) have been shown to be particularly effective in conducting atomistic simulations for such purposes. However, existing NNPs are generally designed for narrow target materials, making them unsuitable for broader applications in material discovery. To overcome this issue, we have developed a universal NNP called PreFerred Potential (PFP), which is able to handle any combination of 45 elements.
matched_sections.reason: The paper addresses the problem of existing NNPs being designed for narrow target materials and proposes a universal NNP for material discovery, which aligns with the user's goal of simulating molecular dynamics for specific materials (lithium-ion battery cathode materials) using NNPs.

matched_sections.paper_title: Towards Universal Neural Network Potential for Material Discovery Applicable to Arbitrary Combination of 45 Elements
matched_sections.heading: INTRODUCTION
matched_sections.text_snippet: One approach to the problem of material exploration is a quantum chemical simulation, such as a density functional theory (DFT)-based method, because many properties of materials stem from atomistic-level phenomena. However, quantum chemical calculations generally require enormous computational resources, limiting the practical use of this method in material discovery for two reasons. First, phenomena of interest in realworld applications often involve temporal and spatial scales vastly exceeding the limitations of quantum calculations, which are usually several hundreds of atoms at a sub-nanosecond scale.
matched_sections.reason: The paper highlights the computational cost limitations of DFT for material discovery and atomistic simulations, which is the core problem the user's idea aims to address by using NNPs 'without full DFT at each timestep'.

matched_sections.paper_title: Towards Universal Neural Network Potential for Material Discovery Applicable to Arbitrary Combination of 45 Elements
matched_sections.heading: Neural network architecture
matched_sections.text_snippet: The TeaNet[58] architecture was used for the base NNP architecture of the PFP. The TeaNet architecture incorporates a 2nd-order Euclidean tensor into the GNN and performs message passing of scalar, vector, and tensor values to represent higher-order geometric features while maintaining the necessary equivariances.
matched_sections.reason: The user explicitly mentions 'message-passing architecture' for the neural network potential, and the paper details the use of the TeaNet architecture, which is a GNN performing 'message passing'.

matched_sections.paper_title: EDIS: A Simulation Software for Dynamic Ion Intercalation/Deintercalation Processes in Electrode Materials
matched_sections.heading: Abstract
matched_sections.text_snippet: To address these challenges, we have developed the Electrode Dynamic Ion Intercalation/Deintercalation Simulator (EDIS), a software platform designed to simulate the dynamic processes of ion intercalation and deintercalation in electrode materials. Leveraging high-precision machine learning potentials, EDIS can efficiently model structural evolution and lithium-ion diffusion behavior under various states of charge and discharge, achieving accuracy approaching that of quantum mechanical methods in relevant chemical spaces.
matched_sections.reason: The paper addresses the challenge of simulating dynamic processes in electrode materials, specifically focusing on ion intercalation and deintercalation, which aligns with the user's goal of simulating molecular dynamics for lithium-ion battery cathode materials.

matched_sections.paper_title: EDIS: A Simulation Software for Dynamic Ion Intercalation/Deintercalation Processes in Electrode Materials
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: For example, first-principles methods offer high accuracy but are computationally expensive and cannot address long time-scale processes under practical conditions. Classical molecular dynamics is efficient but limited by the accuracy of empirical force fields. To address these challenges, we have developed the Electrode Dynamic Ion Intercalation/Deintercalation Simulator (EDIS). EDIS leverages machine learning potential technology, combining the accuracy of first-principles calculations with the efficiency of molecular dynamics to enable precise and efficient simulation as well as in-depth analysis of the dynamic charging/discharging processes in electrode materials.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states the problem of computational expense for first-principles methods in long time-scale processes and the limitations of empirical force fields, which is the underlying motivation for the user's idea of simulating molecular dynamics 'without full DFT at each timestep'.

Herein, we utilize NequIP model and the structural relaxation data from Materials Project to construct robust, precise and universal force fields for Mg cathode materials, and employ it to screen ionic conductivity for high-voltage cathodes materials selected in the previous section[11].
matched_sections.reason: The paper describes using a NequIP model (a neural network potential with a message-passing architecture) as a machine learning force field for MD simulations, which is directly comparable to the user's 'training a neural network potential using a message-passing architecture...to simulate molecular dynamics'. The use of 'structural relaxation data from Materials Project' is analogous to 'DFT-labeled atomic configurations'.

matched_sections.paper_title: AI-Driven Accelerated Discovery of Intercalation-type Cathode Materials for Magnesium Batteries
matched_sections.heading: Computational Details
matched_sections.text_snippet: We utilize Density Functional Theory (DFT) to calculate voltages of Mg cathode materials[21]. All density functional calculations are performed using the Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP)[22].
matched_sections.reason: The paper uses DFT calculations for validating predictions and generating data, which is similar to the user's 'DFT-labeled atomic configurations' for training the potential.

matched_sections.paper_title: AI-Driven Accelerated Discovery of Intercalation-type Cathode Materials for Magnesium Batteries
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: Magnesium-ion batteries hold promise as future energy storage solution, yet current Mg cathodes are challenged by low voltage and specific capacity. Herein, we present an AI-driven workflow for discovering high-performance Mg cathode materials.
matched_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper focus on cathode materials for ion batteries. While the user specifies 'lithium-ion battery cathode materials', the paper focuses on 'Magnesium-ion batteries' and 'Mg cathode materials', which are closely related within the broader domain of battery materials.

matched_sections.paper_title: AI-Driven Accelerated Discovery of Intercalation-type Cathode Materials for Magnesium Batteries
matched_sections.heading: Introduction
matched_sections.text_snippet: Magnesium-ion batteries (MIBs), using an earth-abundant, safe Mg anode of high volumetric capacity (3833 mAhcm -3 , versus 2046 mAhcm -3 of Li anode), have received widespread attention[1, 2]. However, searching advanced magnesium cathode materials with improved voltage, energy density, and fast ion transport capability is urgently needed.
matched_sections.reason: The paper discusses 'magnesium cathode materials' and also mentions 'lithium-ion batteries (LIBs)' as a related area where similar methods have been successful, indicating a strong domain overlap in battery cathode materials.

matched_sections.paper_title: AI-Driven Accelerated Discovery of Intercalation-type Cathode Materials for Magnesium Batteries
matched_sections.heading: ABSTRACT
matched_sections.text_snippet: We further train a precise NequIP model to facilitate accurate and rapid simulations of Mg ionic conductivity. From the 160 high voltage structures, the machine learning molecular dynamics simulations have selected 23 cathode materials with both high energy density and high ionic conductivity.
matched_sections.reason: The paper's contribution of using MLMD simulations (based on a NequIP model, a type of neural network potential) to screen for ionic conductivity in cathode materials is very similar to the user's proposed contribution of simulating molecular dynamics for cathode materials using a neural network potential.

matched_sections.paper_title: AI-Driven Accelerated Discovery of Intercalation-type Cathode Materials for Magnesium Batteries
matched_sections.heading: Conclusion
matched_sections.text_snippet: By training a NequIP model to facilitate accurate and rapid MLMD simulations for Mg ionic conductivity screening, we have further identified 23 Mg cathode materials that have both high energy density and high ionic conductivity.
matched_sections.reason: The paper explicitly states the contribution of using a NequIP model for rapid MLMD simulations for ionic conductivity screening, which directly aligns with the user's goal of simulating molecular dynamics for cathode materials without full DFT at each timestep.

sentence_annotations.sentence: Training a neural network potential using a message-passing architecture on DFT-labeled atomic configurations to simulate molecular dynamics for lithium-ion battery cathode materials without full DFT at each timestep.

linked_sections.paper_title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: The core of molecular dynamics simulation fundamentally lies in the interatomic potential. Traditional empirical potentials lack accuracy, while first-principles methods are computationally prohibitive. Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) promise near-quantum accuracy at linear cost, but existing models still face challenges in efficiency and stability.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper address the problem of simulating molecular dynamics with high accuracy and efficiency, specifically by overcoming the computational limitations of first-principles methods like DFT.

linked_sections.paper_title: Towards Universal Neural Network Potential for Material Discovery Applicable to Arbitrary Combination of 45 Elements
linked_sections.heading: Neural network architecture
linked_sections.text_snippet: The TeaNet[58] architecture was used for the base NNP architecture of the PFP. The TeaNet architecture incorporates a 2nd-order Euclidean tensor into the GNN and performs message passing of scalar, vector, and tensor values to represent higher-order geometric features while maintaining the necessary equivariances.
linked_sections.reason: The user explicitly mentions 'message-passing architecture' for the neural network potential, and the paper details the use of the TeaNet architecture, which is a GNN performing 'message passing'.

linked_sections.paper_title: EDIS: A Simulation Software for Dynamic Ion Intercalation/Deintercalation Processes in Electrode Materials
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: As the core determinant of lithium-ion battery performance, electrode materials play a crucial role in defining the battery's capacity, cycling stability, and durability. During charging and discharging, electrode materials undergo complex ion intercalation and deintercalation processes, accompanied by defect formation and structural evolution.
linked_sections.reason: Both the user's idea and the paper are explicitly focused on 'lithium-ion battery cathode materials' and the underlying processes in 'electrode materials'.

linked_sections.paper_title: Universal and efficient graph neural networks with dynamic attention for machine learning interatomic potentials
linked_sections.heading: Abstract
linked_sections.text_snippet: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) promise near-quantum accuracy at linear cost, but existing models still face challenges in efficiency and stability. We presents Machine Learning Advances Neural Network (MLANet), an efficient and robust graph neural network framework. MLANet introduces a dual-path dynamic attention mechanism for geometry-aware message passing and a multi-perspective pooling strategy to construct comprehensive system representations. This design enables highly accurate modeling of atomic environments while achieving exceptional computational efficiency, making high-fidelity simulations more accessible.
linked_sections.reason: The user's idea aims to enable molecular dynamics simulations for lithium-ion battery materials 'without full DFT at each timestep', implying a contribution of efficiency and accuracy. The paper's MLANet also claims to provide 'near-quantum accuracy at linear cost' and 'exceptional computational efficiency' for MD simulations.

To improve originality, focus on differentiating your core methodology and clearly articulating a novel contribution distinct from current research in AI-driven material discovery and graph neural networks.
